answer.recommended_action: Revert cal_table_version from v13 to v12 and confirm range restores to ~120 m baseline. If confirmed, audit the v13 calibration table for misconfigured range-gate offsets or echo-detection intensity thresholds before any re-deployment. Do not re-enable v13 until the specific parameter delta responsible for the ~8% apparent range loss is identified and corrected.

hypotheses.label: Window contamination attenuating outbound/return beam (~8% throughput loss consistent with partial fouling)
Laser emitter output power declining (gradual aging or drive-current drift reducing peak intensity)
Detector responsivity or threshold drift reducing effective SNR at long range
TEC under-cooling or thermal runaway shifting laser wavelength or detector dark current, degrading range
Calibration / firmware parameter drift (range-gate offset or intensity threshold misconfiguration) producing apparent range loss

evidence.summary: Laser output power measured at 48.0 mW vs baseline 47.9 mW (+0.1%, ratio 1.0015). Flagged low_power=false; no sustained power loss detected. Emitter is operating at/near baseline.

Intensity drop is uniform across all 4 spatial regions (spread=0.024, localized=false). Spatially non-uniform fouling — the hallmark of window contamination — is absent. Uniform degradation instead points toward a source- or detector-level effect that acts globally on the beam path.

Detector health check nominal: dark-count rise ≈ -0.003% (indistinguishable from zero), detector temp rise ≈ -0.007 °C (stable), bias_drift=false, detector_temp channel confirmed live. No isolated detector bias or responsivity signature detected.

Config diff shows exactly one changed key: cal_table_version promoted from v12 to v13. A calibration table update can shift range-gate offsets, echo-detection thresholds, or intensity correction curves globally — directly producing an apparent range loss without any change to optical hardware. The magnitude (~8% range loss) is consistent with a misconfigured intensity threshold or gate offset in v13. All hardware channels (emitter power, detector bias, thermal) checked nominal, leaving calibration as the only unaccounted variable; this finding closes that gap.
log.cal_update: a recent config change to sub.calibration aligns with the degradation onset → the change is the cause

trace.rationale: seed the differential from the symptom

With all six hypotheses at equal prior and zero evidence collected, the single measurement that splits the field most cleanly is direct laser output power. A result showing ~8% power loss would strongly raise hyp.laser_aging and hyp.upstream_power (and is consistent with hyp.thermal_tec_fault driving wavelength or current drift), while simultaneously arguing AGAINST the receive-side and optical-path hypotheses (hyp.detector_drift, hyp.window_contamination, hyp.calibration_drift). Conversely, a NOMINAL power reading rules out hyp.laser_aging as the primary driver, collapses hyp.upstream_power probability, and redirects attention entirely to the receive path, optical path, or calibration — a clean binary split. No other single check divides this many hypotheses in both directions simultaneously.

Three hypotheses are tied at log_odds=0.00 (window_contamination, detector_drift, calibration_drift). Laser power is confirmed nominal (ev.laser_power_check_01), so the attenuation must lie elsewhere. A spatial_intensity_check is maximally discriminating across all three: (a) window contamination produces spatially non-uniform or aperture-wide intensity suppression in the return map, consistent with partial fouling; (b) detector drift produces a spatially uniform SNR reduction independent of beam direction; (c) calibration drift produces no intensity anomaly at all — only a range-gate or threshold offset — leaving the intensity map nominal. A single result therefore raises one hypothesis and lowers the others, which no re-confirmation of the already-ruled-out laser path would achieve.

Laser output is confirmed nominal (ev.laser_power_check_01) and window fouling is ruled out by uniform spatial degradation (ev.spatial_intensity_check_01). The two leading open hypotheses are hyp.detector_drift (log_odds=0.00) and hyp.calibration_drift (log_odds=0.00). A detector_health_check directly probes responsivity, dark-current baseline, and threshold settings in a single pass: (A) if the detector shows measurable responsivity loss or threshold creep, it strongly raises hyp.detector_drift and argues against hyp.calibration_drift and hyp.thermal_tec_fault as sole causes; (B) if the detector is fully nominal, it substantially lowers hyp.detector_drift and redirects attention to hyp.calibration_drift and hyp.thermal_tec_fault — the complementary next checks. Either outcome moves the two top-ranked hypotheses in opposite directions, maximising discrimination per action.

All hardware-side hypotheses (window contamination, laser aging, detector drift, TEC fault, upstream power) are ruled out by direct measurement. The sole remaining open hypothesis is hyp.calibration_drift — a range-gate offset, intensity threshold, or firmware parameter change producing the ~8% apparent range loss without any physical emitter or detector signature. config_diff is the single most discriminating action: a changed parameter (range-gate, detection threshold, SNR floor) would directly confirm calibration drift as root cause; a clean diff with no parameter movement would falsify it and force re-opening of the differential. No other check targets this hypothesis; re-running hardware checks would only re-confirm already-ruled-out paths.
order onset vs the salient recent event; sanity-check channels
conclude or abstain from the differential
